attributes.stem:
A 72-year-old woman presents to the General Practice clinic with concerns about a gradually developing blue-grey discolouration of her face and backs of her hands over the past eight months. She has a history of atrial fibrillation, hypertension, and dyslipidaemia. Her medications include amiodarone, warfarin, perindopril, and atorvastatin. She is a retired surfer and walks daily on the beach.

On examination, she appears well with vital signs within normal limits. There is symmetrical blue-grey hyperpigmentation on sun-exposed areas including her face and dorsal aspects of her hands. The pigmentation is not tender or raised. The rest of her skin examination notes multiple solar keratosis on her hands and face, as well as a rolled lesion suspicious of BCC.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most appropriate management for the new blue-grey skin changes?
attributes.explanation:
This patient presents with classic features of amiodarone-induced cutaneous hyperpigmentation, which typically develops in sun-exposed areas anytime on therapy. The condition is caused by the accumulation of lipofuscin granules in dermal macrophages and is more common in fair-skinned individuals with significant sun exposure.

Clinical surveillance is appropriate if the patient is clinically stable on amiodarone and the cosmetic effects are acceptable to the patient. The pigmentation is benign and does not indicate toxicity, though patients should be advised about sun protection measures. The effectiveness of amiodarone is not altered by the dermatological side effect.

Immediate cessation of amiodarone would be inappropriate as this could destabilise the patient's cardiac rhythm. Any changes to amiodarone therapy should be made in consultation with the patient's cardiologist, weighing the benefits of the medication against cosmetic concerns.

Topical hydroquinone cream would be ineffective as the pigmentation is located in the dermis rather than the epidermis. The pigmentation will gradually fade if amiodarone is discontinued, though this may take months to years.

Excisional biopsy is unnecessary as the diagnosis can be made clinically based on the characteristic appearance and medication history. Biopsy would leave unnecessary scarring without changing management.

Topical high-dose corticosteroid cream would be ineffective for amiodarone-induced pigmentation as it does not address the underlying mechanism of lipofuscin accumulation in dermal macrophages. Additionally, long-term use of high-potency topical steroids can cause skin atrophy and other adverse effects.

Patient education about sun protection is important to prevent progression. If the cosmetic appearance becomes unacceptable to the patient, discussion with their cardiologist about alternative antiarrhythmic medications could be considered or reduction to less than 200mg amiodarone may help to reverse the pigmentation over time.
learningPoints: Amiodarone-induced skin hyperpigmentation typically presents as blue-grey discolouration in sun-exposed areas.
Amiodarone-induced pigmentation may persist for months to years after drug discontinuation due to its long half-life.
Management depends on clinical necessity of amiodarone therapy and severity of cosmetic concerns.
references: Samadi Takaldani, A.H., Negaresh, M., Salimi, M. et al. Amiodarone-induced blue man syndrome: a case report. Journal of Medical Case Reports 17, 248 .2023. Available at: https://jmedicalcasereports.biomedcentral.com/articles/10.1186/s13256-023-03954-6

Orgaz-Molina J, Giron-Prieto M, Cotugno M, Arias-Santiago S. Facial discolouration A case study. Australian Family Physician. 2013: 42 (5). Available at: https://www.racgp.org.au/afp/2013/may/facial-discolouration
